MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination Of Jane Doe
Off Calendar Mar-17-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS SUTTER HEALTH, and SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS', DBA CALIFORNIA PACIFIC, Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination Of Jane Doe.

Appearances:
Leora J. Perkowski, Esq., Law Office of, via Zoom Videoconference, (209) 373-0069, for Plaintiffs.
Gregory Lewis, Esq., of Hassard Bonnington, Esq., in person, (415) 288-9800, for Defendants.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants Sutter Health and Sutter Bay Hospitals' motion to compel independent medical examination of plaintiff Jane Doe is denied. Jane Doe correctly argues that this motion cannot be granted without the moving parties obtaining leave to reopen discovery and their belated ex parte application to do so has been denied.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions
Off Calendar Mar-17-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFFS JANE DOE, and JOHN DOE'S Motion For Sanctions.

Appearances:
Leora J. Perkowski, Esq., Law Office of, via Zoom Videoconference, (209) 373-0069, for Plaintiffs.
Gregory Lewis, Esq., of Hassard Bonnington, Esq., in person, (415) 288-9800, for Defendants.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's motion for sanctions is denied. Plaintiffs have not shown that the Sutter defendants willfully violated a court order or that they suffered any prejudice that would be remediated by the requested sanctions.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Additional Time To Complete The Deposition Of Plaintiff Jane Doe
Off Calendar Mar-17-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS SUTTER HEALTH, and SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS', DBA CALIFORNIA PACIFIC, Motion To Compel Additional Time To Complete The Deposition Of Plaintiff Jane Doe.

Appearances:
Leora J. Perkowski, Esq., Law Office of, via Zoom Videoconference, (209) 373-0069, for Plaintiffs.
Gregory Lewis, Esq., of Hassard Bonnington, Esq., in person, (415) 288-9800, for Defendants.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants Sutter Health and Sutter Bay Hospitals' motion to compel additional time to complete the deposition of plaintiff Jane Doe is granted in part. Defendants may further depose plaintiff Jane Doe for no more than four hours, excluding breaks and attorney colloquy. The further session of the deposition must occur on a mutually convenient date no later than March 26, 2026. The Sutter defendants have shown good cause for additional time to depose Jane Doe and her arguments opposing additional time lack merit. Due to the imminence of the trial and the Sutter defendants' delay in seeking additional time, the last session of Jane Doe's deposition is limited to four hours.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)

HEARING ON DEFENDANTS' APPLICATION SEEKING AN ORDER TO REOPEN DISCOVERY, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, AN ORDER SHORTENING TIME FOR A HEARING ON DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO REOPEN DISCOVERY
Off Calendar Mar-17-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: HEARING ON DEFENDANTS' EX PARTE APPLICATION SEEKING AN ORDER TO REOPEN DISCOVERY, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, AN ORDER SHORTENING TIME FOR A HEARING ON DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO REOPEN DISCOVERY.

Appearances:
Leora J. Perkowski, Esq., Law Office of, via Zoom Videoconference, (209) 373-0069, for Plaintiffs.
Gregory Lewis, Esq., of Hassard Bonnington, Esq., in person, (415) 288-9800, for Defendants.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants Sutter Health and Sutter Bay Hospitals' ex parte application to reopen discovery or, in the alternative, for an order shortening time for a hearing on a motion to reopen discovery is denied in its entirety. This case has been pending too long and the trial is too imminent to allow for a hearing on a motion to conduct an eleventh and a half hour independent medical examination of plaintiff Jane Doe which would be severely prejudicial to Jane Doe because she did not designate an expert to rebut the opinions resulting from that examination and may not even receive the results of the examination and the opinions therefrom until just before trial or possibly even after the start of trial. In short, defendants fail to adequately justify their delay in seeking an IME of Jane Doe and Jane Doe has provided an ample showing that she would be prejudiced by the allowing of an IME at this stage of the case. The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's motion to compel depositions of Sharon Patrick and Yuan-Da Fan is granted in part. Plaintiffs may take a deposition of Dr. Fan and Ms. Patrick of no more than three hours, excluding breaks and attorney colloquy, of each deponent that must occur on a mutually convenient date no later than March 26, 2026. Plaintiffs have shown good cause for these depositions and the Sutter defendants' arguments opposing the depositions lack merit. Due to the imminence of the trial and plaintiffs' delay in noticing the depositions, the depositions will be limited to three hours each. Because the Sutter defendants did not act without substantial justification, plaintiffs' request for sanctions is denied.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/HEK)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-01-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):

Julie D. Cantor, Esq. (424) 291-2194, Law Office for Plaintiff.

Gregory Lewis, Esq. (415) 288-9800, HASSARD BONNINGTON LLP for Defendant.

Sarah Gosling, Esq. SCHUERING ZIMMERMAN & DOYLE, LLP (916) 567.0400, for Defendant.

Matter not Contested/Argued. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant ANNA KOGAN, M.D.'S demurrer to Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's fifth amended complaint ("5AC") is sustained without leave to amend:

Abuse of dependent adult:

"Abandonment" means the desertion or willful forsaking of an elder or a dependent adult by anyone having care or custody of that person under circumstances in which a reasonable person would continue to provide care and custody. (Welf. & Inst. Code 15610.05.)

Here, Plaintiff Jane Doe fails to allege abuse of a dependent adult with respect to Defendant Kogan. (5AC, 99-105.) Plaintiff alleges abandonment against Defendant because she was the attending physician and failed to provide oversight because she did not meet Plaintiff during her stay. (Id. at 19-102.) Plaintiff fails to cite any law that states attending physicians are required to meet patients face to face while overseeing their care. Plaintiff has not adequately pleaded that Defendant did not act reasonably, in light of the circumstances. Sustained with leave without to amend as this defect cannot be cured.

Intentional infliction of emotional distress ("IIED"):

To state a claim for IIED, "a plaintiff must show: (1) outrageous conduct by the defendant; (2) the defendant's intention of causing or reckless disregard of the probability of causing emotional distress; (3) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress; and (4) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct. Conduct, to be 'outrageous' must be so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. In order to avoid a demurrer, the plaintiff must allege with 'great[ ] specificity' the acts which he or she believes are so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Yau v. Santa Margarita Ford, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th 144, 160-61 (internal quotations and citations omitted).)

Here, Plaintiffs fail to allege the elements of IIED with respect to Defendant. (5AC 108-15.) Plaintiffs failed to specifically allege acts so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community. (Id. at 11:15-20, 112:13-17.) Defendant never met Plaintiff and Defendant and her failing to meet Plaintiffs' expectations during her hospital stay does not rise to that level. (Id.) at Sustained without leave to amend as this defect cannot be cured.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B, Ulmer Jr., Clerk: R. Woods, Court Reporter: Tanya Cunha, CSR: 13788, E: cunhareporting@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

The Court shares its views (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) Upon conclusion of oral argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Garrett's demurrer to the fifth amended complaint ("5AC") is sustained without leave to amend as to the IIED claim. Plaintiffs state that Defendant Garrett was removed from the medical battery and abuse of a dependent adult causes of action. (Opp. 2:8.) Therefore, the demurrer is unnecessary as to those causes of action. (Opp. 2:8.)
Intentional infliction of emotional distress ("IIED"): To state a claim for IIED, "a plaintiff must show: (1) outrageous conduct by the defendant; (2) the defendant's intention of causing or reckless disregard of the probability of causing emotional distress; (3) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress; and (4) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct. Conduct, to be 'outrageous' must be so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. In order to avoid a demurrer, the plaintiff must allege with 'great[ ] specificity' the acts which he or she believes are so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Yau v. Santa Margarita Ford, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th 144, 160-61 (internal quotations and citations omitted).)

Here, Plaintiffs fail to allege the elements of IIED with respect to Defendant. Plaintiffs fail to specifically allege acts so extreme, as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community. (5AC, 108-114.) Sustained without leave to amend.

Taken Under Submission.***

Matter not Contested/Argued. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant VANESSA EVERS, CNM's demurrer to Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's fifth amended complaint ("5AC") is sustained without leave to amend:

Abuse of dependent adult:

"Abandonment" means the desertion or willful forsaking of an elder or a dependent adult by anyone having care or custody of that person under circumstances in which a reasonable person would continue to provide care and custody. (Welf. & Inst. Code 15610.05.)

Here, Plaintiff Jane Doe fails to allege abuse of a dependent adult with respect to Defendant Evers. (5AC, 99-105.) MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):

Julie D. Cantor, Esq. (424) 291-2194, Law Office for Plaintiff.

Gregory Lewis, Esq. (415) 288-9800, HASSARD BONNINGTON LLP for Defendant.

Sarah Gosling, Esq. SCHUERING ZIMMERMAN & DOYLE, LLP (916) 567.0400, for Defendant.

Matter not Contested/Argued. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant LILY PEMBERTON, M.D.'S demurrer to Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's fifth amended complaint ("5AC") is sustained without leave to amend:

Abuse of dependent adult:

"Abandonment" means the desertion or willful forsaking of an elder or a dependent adult by anyone having care or custody of that person under circumstances in which a reasonable person would continue to provide care and custody. (Welf. & Inst. Code 15610.05.)

Here, Plaintiff Jane Doe fails to allege abuse of a dependent adult with respect to Defendant Pemberton. (5AC, 99-105.) Plaintiff alleges abandonment against Defendant because she did not speak to Plaintiff before she approved Defendant Evers decision to send Plaintiff home until her labor progressed enough to warrant hospital admission. (Id. at 101:4-8.) Plaintiff has not adequately pleaded that Defendant did not act reasonably, in light of the circumstances. Sustained without leave to amend as this defect cannot be cured.

Intentional infliction of emotional distress ("IIED"):

To state a claim for IIED, "a plaintiff must show: (1) outrageous conduct by the defendant; (2) the defendant's intention of causing or reckless disregard of the probability of causing emotional distress; (3) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress; and (4) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct. Conduct, to be 'outrageous' must be so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. In order to avoid a demurrer, the plaintiff must allege with 'great[ ] specificity' the acts which he or she believes are so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Yau v. Santa Margarita Ford, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th 144, 160-61 (internal quotations and citations omitted).)

Here, Plaintiffs fail to allege the elements of IIED with respect to Defendant. (5AC, 108-115.) Plaintiffs failed to specifically allege acts so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community. (Id. at 108-115.) Defendant failing to meet Plaintiff's expectations for the experience she wanted while at the hospital (Id. at 110-112) does not rise to that level. Sustained without leave to amend as this defect cannot be cured.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Motion To Seal The Docket, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities, Declaration Of Julie D. Cantor Md Jd And Exhibits, And Proposed Order Filed Concurrently Herewith
Off Calendar Dec-22-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Julie Cantor, Esq., J. CANTOR LAW, (424) 291-2194, jc@jcantorlaw.com, Attorney for Plaintiffs Jane Doe et al.
Gregory J. Lewis, Esq., HASSARD BONNINGTON LLP, (415) 288-9800, gjl@hassard.com, Attorneys for Defendants SUTTER HEALTH, ELIZABETH GARRETT, RN and MABELBA OGUNDELE, RN
Andrew J. Goldman, Esq., THE GOLDMAN LAW FIRM, (415) 435-5500, sundance@goldmanlawfirm.net, Attorneys for Defendants ANNA KOGAN, M.D.,et al,
Michelle Wilson, CSR# 14303, 707-761-6001, michellewilsoncrs@gmail.com, Court Reporter

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFFS JANE DOE and JOHN DOE'S Motion To Seal The Docket.

Plaintiffs' motion to seal the docket is denied. The First Amendment provides a right of access to civil trials and proceedings, including civil litigation documents filed in court as a basis for adjudication. (NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Super. Ct. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1208-1209 & fn. 25.) Under rules 2.550 and 2.551 of the California Rules of Court, the court may order a record sealed only upon making express findings that (1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 2.550(d); Overstock.com, Inc. v. Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (2014) 231 Cal.App.4th 471, 487.) Due to the important constitutional principles at stake in open proceedings in all civil cases, the proponent of a sealing order must make a "specific showing of serious injury" that it would suffer if the documents are made available to the public. (Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 1273, 1282.)

Plaintiffs do not meet their burden under California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551 to show that the Court should seal any particular document, much less the entire court docket. There is no showing that any portion of the docket contains Plaintiffs' names, birth dates, or other identifying information. Plaintiffs' primary concern appears to stem from the issuance of subpoenas for their medical records, which are not typically filed with the court. Sealing the docket is not narrowly tailored and would not directly address Plaintiffs' purported privacy concern: that third parties with access to the subpoena could access the court file and learn their identities, thereby invading their privacy. Plaintiffs are proceeding in this action under "Doe" pseudonyms. Moreover, any healthcare provider or other third person who receives a subpoena for Plaintiffs' medical records will already know Plaintiffs' identities and have access to records showing the nature of the medical conditions for which they sought treatment.

Counsel for defendant Sutter Health is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC3.1312.

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-22-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SUTTER HEALTH DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant Sutter Health's demurrer to Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's second amended complaint ("SAC") is sustained without leave to amend as to Negligent hiring, supervision, and retention, and sustained with leave to amend Violation of the Ralph Civil Rights Act of 1976:

Negligent hiring, supervision, and retention: Sustained without leave to amend as conceded by John Doe.

Violation of the Ralph Civil Rights Act of 1976:

"Under the Ralph Act, a plaintiff must establish the defendant threatened or committed violent acts against the plaintiff or their property, and a motivating reason for doing so was a prohibited discriminatory motive, or that the defendant aided, incited, or conspired in the denial of a protected right." (Gabrielle A. v. Cnty. of Orange (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 1268, 1291.) "There can be no 'threat of violence' without some expression of intent to injure or damage plaintiffs or their property." (Ramirez v. Wong (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 1480, 1486.)

Plaintiff Jane Doe fails to allege Sutter Health's employees committed a violation of the Ralph Civil Rights Act of 1976. Plaintiff failed to plead that Sutter Health's employees expressed an intent to injure or damage her or that they committed violent acts motivated by her sex, medical condition, appearance or accent, or that they aided, incited, or conspired in the denial of a protected right. (SAC, 122-124). Sustained with leave to amend.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-22-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MABELBA OGUNDELE, RN DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant Mabelba Ogundele, RN's demurrer to Plaintiffs Jane and John Doe's second amended complaint ("SAC") is sustained with leave to amend:

Intentional infliction of emotional distress ("IIED"):

To state a claim for IIED, "a plaintiff must show: (1) outrageous conduct by the defendant; (2) the defendant's intention of causing or reckless disregard of the probability of causing emotional distress; (3) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress; and (4) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct. Conduct, to be 'outrageous' must be so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. In order to avoid a demurrer, the plaintiff must allege with 'great[ ] specificity' the acts which he or she believes are so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Yau v. Santa Margarita Ford, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th 144, 160-61 (internal quotations and citations omitted).)

Here, Plaintiffs fail to allege a claim for IIED with respect to Defendant. Plaintiffs fail to specifically allege outrageous conduct by Defendant that was so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized society. (SAC, 107-108.) Sustained with leave to amend.

Violation of the Ralph Civil Rights Act of 1976:

"Under the Ralph Act, a plaintiff must establish the defendant threatened or committed violent acts against the plaintiff or their property, and a motivating reason for doing so was a prohibited discriminatory motive, or that the defendant aided, incited, or conspired in the denial of a protected right." (Gabrielle A. v. Cnty. of Orange (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 1268, 1291.) "There can be no 'threat of violence' without some expression of intent to injure or damage plaintiffs or their property." (Ramirez v. Wong (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 1480, 1486.)

Plaintiff Jane Doe fails to allege Defendant committed a violation of the Ralph Civil Rights Act of 1976. Plaintiff failed to plead that Defendant expressed an intent to injure or damage her or that Defendant committed violent acts motivated by her sex, medical condition, appearance or accent, or that Defendant aided, incited, or conspired in the denial of a protected right. (SAC, 122-124.) Sustained with leave to amend.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
